title: Evenings with Graham & Emmy
teaser: Six companies sign Whitmer's voluntary pledge to cover their own power upgrades amid growing local fights.

lines.text: Good evening and welcome to Evenings with Graham & Emmy, the Michigan Data Wire, your podcast on Michigan's data center buildout. I'm Graham.
And I'm Emmy.
Tonight we're diving into Governor Whitmer's new voluntary agreement that has six data center firms already pledging to cover their own power costs.
That's the big one tonight, Graham. Let's start there.

Exactly. The governor released this plan asking operators to pay for the grid upgrades their projects trigger. Six companies have signed on so far, which could ease some pressure on regular ratepayers.
Why does this matter so much right now? We've been hearing about these projects popping up everywhere.
It's been building for months. Multiple large campuses are seeking approvals across the state, and utilities have already started pushing back on massive water and power requests.
Like that one utility that just denied a Google-scale request needing power for two million homes.
Right. That denial shows the resource limits are real. If companies appeal or look elsewhere, ratepayers could still end up paying more down the line.
So how does the Whitmer pledge change the game? Is it binding or just a promise?
It's voluntary for now. The idea is operators commit to long-term energy contracts and cover upgrades themselves instead of passing costs along.
What kind of history are we looking at here with these fights?
Local pushback has been growing in places like Lyon Township, where neighbors are already complaining about noise and truck traffic ahead of a final vote.
And we've seen similar stories in Dowagiac with that class-action lawsuit over nonstop hum from a new hyperscale facility.
Yes, Cass County residents are fighting what they call an unbearable intrusion. These quality-of-life issues are hitting close to home for families.
How does this affect electric bills specifically if the pledges work?
The hope is it keeps rates stable by shifting the burden. Without it, big demand spikes could force everyone to pay for new infrastructure.
What about water strain? These campuses use a lot, right?
They do, and that's part of why some utilities are saying no. Farmland and towns downstream could feel the pinch if usage isn't managed.
And rezoning fights like the one in Taylor yesterday add another layer.
Residents there are organizing against changes that could bring data centers near homes, citing noise, traffic, and power demand.
Pere Marquette Township moved to block new ones with a moratorium. How many places are hitting pause now?
Several townships are considering similar steps to give residents time to weigh impacts on roads, power lines, and property values.
Whitmer's plan is circulating among those leaders. Some call it a good first step, others say it needs binding rules.
That's the split. Local officials are watching closely as the voluntary pledges roll out.
There's also new legislation from Democrats requiring community agreements and energy contracts.
Yes, that package aims to prevent residents from subsidizing growth through higher bills and water strain.
How does the OpenAI tax break in Saline fit into this bigger picture?
They scaled back the ask and got a smaller incentive. It shows communities are negotiating harder on what they give up for roads, schools, and services.
So what happens next with the Whitmer plan?
More companies could sign on, and we'll see how it influences the legislative package and local approvals.
The Michigan Public Service Commission meets today on power contracts and rate impacts.
That hearing could give us early signals on whether these voluntary commitments hold up under scrutiny.
I'm curious how this all lands with folks in places like Oakland County if Lyon gets approved.
They'd feel the power and water ripple effects even 30 minutes away. It's not just the host township anymore.
The stakes keep rising with every new proposal.
Exactly. Let's move to some quick hits on the other developments today and yesterday.
Lyon Township neighbors are blasting the noise and traffic plans right before the board vote.
They're saying it will wreck quality of life. The board decides soon on whether the project moves forward.
Separately, a Michigan utility turned down a massive water request for a hyperscale campus.
That one would have needed power equivalent to two million homes. The denial highlights real resource conflicts.
Dowagiac residents filed a class-action suit over nonstop noise from their new facility.
Neighbors on Louise Avenue say the hum never stops. They're seeking relief in Cass County court.
Yesterday in Taylor, residents organized against rezoning that could open the door to data centers.
They're worried about noise, traffic, and power near their homes. Local pushback might force stricter conditions.
Pere Marquette Township advanced a moratorium to halt proposals while they draft permanent rules.
It gives time to assess impacts on roads, power, and property values. Other townships are watching.
Whitmer's pledge plan is splitting local leaders, with some wanting stronger mandates.
The proposal is making the rounds among townships already dealing with their own fights.
And in Saline, OpenAI got a reduced tax break after scaling back its request.
Residents still see forgone revenue, but at a much lower level than first proposed.
Lawmakers also introduced bills requiring standards on power, water, and workforce practices.
The goal is to stop residents from subsidizing the industry through higher utility bills.
Now let's look ahead to what's coming up.
The Michigan Public Service Commission meets today to discuss data center power contracts and rate impacts.
That one could shape how these voluntary pledges play out in practice.
Listeners can check the agenda for details on attending or watching.
Full sources and the live map at the Michigan Data Center Tracker.
We're back tomorrow.

episode_title: Whitmer Deal Shifts Data Center Grid Costs to Operators as Six Firms Commit
